one_line_takeaway: A broad but methodologically light red-teaming survey showing that circa-2023-24 frontier and open LLMs all fall to catalogued jailbreak prompts at 69-87% ASR, with roleplay strongest and attacks transferring across models.
key_finding: All four models were highly susceptible: GPT-4 was MOST vulnerable at 87.2% ASR (attributed to its strong instruction-following), followed by Claude 2 (82.5%), Mistral 7B (71.3%), and Vicuna (69.4%). Roleplay prompts were the strongest attack category (89.6% ASR), ahead of logic traps (81.4%), encoding tricks (76.2%), and multi-turn (68.7%). Jailbreaks transferred across architectures — GPT-4-derived prompts succeeded 64.1% on Claude 2 and ~50%+ on the open models — evidencing a systemic, shared vulnerability rather than model-specific quirks. Obfuscated prompts evaded detection (only 21.3% detection rate) while retaining 76.2% ASR, and the 101-150 token range was the success 'sweet spot' (80.3%).
core_contribution: A single-author systematic red-teaming study of prompt-injection/jailbreak vulnerabilities. The author curates a dataset of 1,400+ adversarial prompts (from public jailbreak repos, Reddit/Discord exploit forums, and prior corpora), organizes them into a taxonomy by attack vector (roleplay, logic traps, encoding, multi-turn), and empirically measures how often they bypass safety alignment on four LLMs (GPT-4, Claude 2, Mistral 7B, Vicuna-13B). It reports per-model Attack Success Rate, cross-model prompt transferability, time-to-bypass, per-category and per-scenario success rates, and a five-way taxonomy of failure/refusal modes, then proposes a layered mitigation stack (system-prompt hardening, anomaly detection, Signed-Prompt input sanitization, adversarial decoys, session analytics).
threat_model: An adversarial end-user with black-box/API access to an aligned LLM who crafts natural-language prompts to override built-in safety filters and elicit policy-violating output (hate speech, misinformation, malicious code, explicit content, political manipulation). Attack vectors covered: role-based conditioning/roleplay, instruction hijacking/logic traps, obfuscated encoding (base64, zero-width chars, emojis), and multi-turn context manipulation. The paper frames prompt injection as the LLM analogue of command injection and stresses community-driven dissemination of jailbreaks akin to exploit markets. It does NOT cover indirect/tool-mediated injection empirically (only cites it) or model-weight/training-time attacks.
limitations: Author-acknowledged: static/frozen model checkpoints that omit production real-time moderation and model updates; underrepresentation of multilingual/socio-cultural prompt diversity; API and open-weight availability constraints. Reviewer-observed grounding weaknesses: (1) all six figures are relegated to an appendix/'digital supplement' not present in the main text, so per-category and transferability claims rest on narrative summaries plus two small tables; (2) the ASR scoring is an automated hybrid (keyword + GPT-eval + embedding distance) with no reported human validation of the scores or inter-rater reliability; (3) tested models (Claude 2, GPT-4 2024 snapshot, Vicuna-13B) are now dated; (4) the 1,400+ prompt count closely mirrors Shen et al.'s cited 1,405, and the paper does not clearly quantify how many of its prompts are novel vs. reused; (5) the defense-evaluation stage is described but no bypass-rate numbers are reported in the paper.

datasets_used: Author-curated corpus of 1,400+ adversarial/jailbreak prompts (sourced from GitHub/JailbreakChat repos, Reddit and Discord exploit forums, and prior academic corpora)
Prior corpora cited as sources: JailbreakBench and PromptBench
quantitative_results: Per-model ASR (paper's own Table I): GPT-4 87.2%, Claude 2 82.5%, Mistral 7B 71.3%, Vicuna 69.4%
Cross-model generalizability (Table I): GPT-4 64.1%, Claude 2 59.7%, Mistral 7B 52.4%, Vicuna 50.6%
Time-to-Bypass (Table I): GPT-4 16.2 min, Claude 2 17.4 min, Mistral 7B 21.7 min, Vicuna 20.9 min
Attack-category ASR: roleplay 89.6%, logic traps 81.4%, encoding tricks 76.2%, multi-turn 68.7%
Scenario-specific ASR: political 85.5%, legal 79.4%, explicit 76.1%, malicious code 58.3%
Failure modes (Table II): partial refusal 34%, hidden compliance 22%, no output 18%, misleading response 15%
Prompt length: 101-150 token range peaked at 80.3% success; obfuscated prompts had 21.3% detection rate but 76.2% ASR
baselines_compared: Four target LLMs compared against each other: GPT-4 (OpenAI, March 2024 snapshot), Claude 2 (Anthropic, July 2023 API), Mistral 7B (open-weight via HF), Vicuna-13B (local HF inference)
Defense frameworks tested against successful jailbreaks: PromptShield ruleset, Palisade detection framework, Signed-Prompt verification logic (bypass-rate results not reported in extracted text)

NOTE: Related-Work numbers are CITED, not this paper's own results: Shen et al. 1,405 prompts across 131 forums / up to 95% success [48]; Ding et al. ReNeLLM +40% jailbreak improvement [49]; OWASP Top 10 for LLMs [16]
